title: Fixing Incorrect Type Inference in a Generic Type Guard

statement.markdown: A type guard is a function that checks the type of an expression and returns a type predicate. Write a type guard to check if a given object is a string or a number. The type guard should be generic and work with any type that implements the toString() method.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be an object of any type.
statement.outputFormat: The output should be a boolean indicating whether the input object is a string or a number.
statement.notes: The type guard should be implemented using a generic function.

examples.explanation: The input object is a string.
The input object is a number.
The input object is a string, but not a number.

hints.text: Use the typeof operator to check the type of the input object.
Use a generic function to make the type guard work with any type.

editorial.title: Editorial Title
editorial.intuition: A type guard is a function that checks the type of an expression and returns a type predicate. To implement a type guard, we can use the typeof operator to check the type of the input object.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible solution is to use an if-else statement to check the type of the input object.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution is to use a generic function to make the type guard work with any type.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use a type guard with a generic function and the typeof operator.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The type guard is correct because it returns true if the input object is a string or a number and false otherwise.

functionSignature.javascript: function isStringOrNumber<T>(obj: T): obj is string | number {
